title: Agent Tools
description: Learn how Doxa's documentation assistant searches and reads MDX through contentStore-backed tools.
content:
The Doxa assistant is intentionally narrow. It receives a system prompt with the docs file tree, uses tools to find and read documentation content, then submits the final response to a server-side scope guard.

## Tool Surface

| Tool            | Purpose                                                                             |
| :-------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `grep`          | Search documentation content for a pattern and return file paths with line numbers. |
| `cat`           | Read the full raw MDX content for a documentation file.                             |
| `submit_answer` | Submit the final answer for scope and citation validation.                          |

Both tools read through `contentStore`, so they work with the bundled Vite source and the runtime GitHub adapter.

## Agent Workflow

## Data Flow

## What The Assistant Is Told

The system prompt defines strict behavior:

* Answer using only available documentation content.
* Use `grep` first when the right file is uncertain.
* Use `cat` to read full context before answering.
* Use `submit_answer` for every final response.
* Include links in the `/path#section-anchor` format.
* Include code snippets when `aiConfig.codeSnippets` is enabled.
* Classify responses as supported, partial, or unsupported.
* Return a fixed unsupported response when a question is outside the documentation scope.

## Tool Result Example

```text
content-sources/index.mdx:17:Two content adapters ship with Doxa: Vite and GitHub.
content-sources/index.mdx:41:Set CONTENT_SOURCE=github to fetch docs at runtime.
```

The answer should cite the relevant page instead of inventing deployment or configuration details. If the model submits a citation that was not supported by `grep` or `cat`, the guard rejects it and asks the model to try again with documentation-supported content.

## Improving Agent Answers

* Use specific h2 headings that match reader questions.
* Put key terms in frontmatter keywords.
* Keep examples complete.
* Prefer explicit configuration tables over long prose.
* Regenerate search data after content changes.

title: Chat With Docs
description: Configure and understand Doxa's built-in AI chat drawer for documentation-aware answers.
content:
Chat with Docs gives readers a side panel where they can ask questions about the documentation they are reading. The assistant is constrained to the docs corpus, uses tools to search and read MDX before answering, and passes every final response through server-side scope validation.

## Reader Experience

The chat button appears in the navigation when `Settings.features.ai.chat` is enabled. The drawer supports:

* Streaming assistant responses.
* Visible tool-call activity.
* Expandable tool output.
* Conversation history stored in the browser.
* New conversation and delete controls.
* Expanded and compact drawer widths.

## Request Flow

## Enable Chat

## Provider Options

## Failure Modes

| Symptom                           | Likely cause                                   | Fix                                           |
| :-------------------------------- | :--------------------------------------------- | :-------------------------------------------- |
| Chat returns a missing key error  | `AI_API_KEY` is not set                        | Add the provider key to the environment.      |
| Answers miss new pages            | Search data is stale or docs were not deployed | Regenerate search data and redeploy.          |
| The assistant cannot cite a topic | The topic is not documented                    | Add or expand the relevant MDX page.          |
| Tool calls show no matches        | Query terms do not appear in docs              | Improve headings, descriptions, and keywords. |
| General questions are refused     | The topic is outside the docs corpus           | Add the supported details to the docs.        |

title: llms.txt
description: Expose Doxa documentation to external AI tools through llms.txt and llms-full.txt routes.
content:
Doxa exposes AI-native text routes so external assistants and coding tools can discover, read, and cite your documentation.

## Routes

| Route            | Output                                                                           |
| :--------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `/llms.txt`      | A plain-text index of all docs pages with titles, URLs, and descriptions.        |
| `/llms-full.txt` | The full docs corpus concatenated into one plain-text document with source URLs. |

Both routes are generated at request time from `contentStore`, so they follow the active content adapter.

For per-page raw markdown (one source file per request, frontmatter included), see [Raw Markdown Routes](/ai/raw-markdown).

## How It Works

## Example Index Shape

```text showLineNumbers
# doxa

> Beautiful, fast documentation that stays out of your way.

## Docs

- [Overview](https://docs.example.com/overview): See what Doxa Docs can do.
- [Quickstart](https://docs.example.com/quickstart): Install and run Doxa.
```

## Example Full-Corpus Shape

```text showLineNumbers
# doxa

> Beautiful, fast documentation that stays out of your way.

---

## Overview
Source: https://docs.example.com/overview

Doxa is a production-ready documentation template...
```

## When To Use Each Route

| Need                                         | Use                                                         |
| :------------------------------------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------- |
| Let an AI tool discover available pages      | `/llms.txt`                                                 |
| Give an AI tool the full corpus in one fetch | `/llms-full.txt`                                            |
| Cite a specific docs page                    | URLs listed in either route                                 |
| Keep private docs private                    | Do not expose these routes publicly without access controls |

## Verification

title: Raw Markdown Routes
description: Append `.md` to any docs URL to get the page's source MDX as plain text — for AI agents, scripting, and direct copy.
content:
Every docs page is also reachable as raw markdown by appending `.md` to its URL. This gives AI agents and scripts a one-shot way to pull the source for a single page without parsing HTML or fetching the whole `/llms-full.txt` corpus.

## Routes

| Page URL             | Markdown URL            |
| :------------------- | :---------------------- |
| `/quickstart`        | `/quickstart.md`        |
| `/configuration`     | `/configuration.md`     |
| `/ai/chat-with-docs` | `/ai/chat-with-docs.md` |
| `/development/setup` | `/development/setup.md` |

The response body is the exact MDX file from `src/contents/docs/<slug>/index.mdx`, frontmatter included, served as `text/markdown; charset=utf-8`. Unknown slugs return `404 text/plain`.

## How It Works

The Cloudflare worker entry skips the static-asset binding for `.md` paths so the request reaches `src/server.ts`, which calls `tryRawMarkdownResponse` from `src/lib/raw-markdown.ts`. Both vite (default) and github content adapters expose the raw source identically.

## When To Use Each Endpoint

| Need                                             | Use                       |
| :----------------------------------------------- | :------------------------ |
| Fetch the source for one specific page           | `/<slug>.md`              |
| Discover every page (titles, URLs, descriptions) | `/llms.txt`               |
| Pull the entire corpus in one request            | `/llms-full.txt`          |
| Cite a page in a chat response                   | The page URL or `.md` URL |

## Disable The Feature

The feature is on by default. To turn it off, set `features.rawMarkdown` to `false` in `src/settings/main.ts`:

```ts {6} showLineNumbers
export const Settings = {
  features: {
    copyPage: {
      markdown: true,
    },
    rawMarkdown: false,
  },
};
```

When disabled, requests for `/<slug>.md` fall through to the normal page route and render the HTML shell with a "Document Not Found" message — the same behavior as any other unmatched path.

## Verification

title: Code Blocks
description: Show commands, config files, source snippets, line numbers, line highlights, and language alternatives in Doxa docs.
content:
Code examples are one of Doxa's core reading surfaces. Fenced code blocks support language-aware highlighting, optional line numbers, line highlighting, and a copy button.

## Commands

Use `bash` for terminal commands.

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm dev
```

## Config Files

Use `showLineNumbers` on multi-line config examples.

```ts showLineNumbers
export const Settings = {
  site: {
    name: "doxa",
    url: "https://docs.example.com",
  },
  features: {
    ai: {
      chat: true,
      chatWithPage: true,
      floatingInput: true,
    },
    tableOfContents: true,
  },
};
```

## Highlight Important Lines

Line highlighting helps readers find the part that matters.

```ts {6-7} showLineNumbers
export const aiConfig = {
  provider: "openrouter",
  model: "z-ai/glm-4.7:nitro",
  codeSnippets: true,
  maxContextChunks: 12,
  maxResponseTokens: 2048,
};
```

## Titles In Surrounding Text

Code titles are most reliable when you introduce the file path in prose, then show the code below.

`src/lib/components.ts`

```ts showLineNumbers
export const components = {
  Card,
  CardGrid,
  Note,
  Step,
  StepItem,
  Tabs,
  TabsContent,
  TabsList,
  TabsTrigger,
};
```

## Provider Alternatives

Tabs keep related alternatives in one place.

## Complete Examples

Avoid abbreviated code in docs that users are expected to run.

## Code Block Checklist

* Use a language identifier on every fence.
* Use `showLineNumbers` only for examples longer than a few lines.
* Highlight only the lines that matter.
* Put commands in `bash`, plain URLs in `text`, and config in `ts`, `json`, or `yaml`.
* Keep package-manager alternatives in tabs.

title: Diagrams And Math
description: Render Mermaid diagrams and KaTeX math in Doxa for architecture, workflows, relationships, and formulas.
content:
Doxa includes Mermaid for diagrams and KaTeX for mathematical notation. Use them when a visual model or equation clarifies the written explanation.

## Architecture Diagram

## Agent Sequence

## Entity Relationships

## Math Examples

Inline math is useful for small formulas like $$queryWords \ge 3$$.

Use block math for scoring and ranking explanations:

```math
relevance = \frac{title + headings + keywords + content}{\log(length + 1)}
```

```math
latencyBudget = renderTime + searchTime + modelTime
```

## Mermaid Patterns

| Diagram type     | Use it for                                                  |
| :--------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------- |
| Flowchart        | Architecture, routing, deployment, and content pipelines.   |
| Sequence diagram | AI calls, request lifecycles, and handoffs between systems. |
| ER diagram       | Data models and relationships.                              |
| Decision tree    | Configuration choices and troubleshooting paths.            |

## Practical Rule

One good diagram can anchor a complex page. Several decorative diagrams make the page slower to read.

description: Write clean documentation with GitHub-flavored Markdown, tables, checklists, links, blockquotes, images, and math.
content: Doxa supports standard Markdown through MDX, so writers can start with familiar syntax and introduce components only where they add value. ## Text And Links Use normal Markdown for paragraphs, emphasis, inline code, and links. ```md Doxa renders **bold text**, _emphasis_, `inline code`, and [links](/overview). ``` That renders as: Doxa renders **bold text**, *emphasis*, `inline code`, and [links](/overview). ## Lists Use unordered lists for related facts and ordered lists for sequential ideas that do not need the visual Step component. * MDX files are easy to review in pull requests. * Frontmatter powers page metadata. * Headings feed the right-side table of contents. 1. Draft the page. 2. Add examples. 3. Regenerate search data. 4. Review the rendered page. ## Checklists Checklists are useful for operational docs and release readiness. * \[x] Write the page frontmatter. * \[x] Add one concrete example. * \[ ] Verify internal links. * \[ ] Regenerate the search index. ## Tables Tables work well for settings, flags, limits, and comparisons. | Feature | Markdown syntax | When to use | | :--------- | :----------------- | :----------------------------------- | ----- | --- | ---------------------------- | | Checklist | `- [ ] Task` | Launch or review workflows. | | Table | ` | Column | Value |` | Settings and reference data. | | Blockquote | `> Text` | Quotes and short editorial emphasis. | | Code fence | \`\`\`ts | Commands, examples, and config. | ## Blockquotes > Good docs reduce the number of decisions a reader has to hold in memory. Use blockquotes sparingly. If the message is operationally important, use a `Note` instead. ## Images Images use standard Markdown syntax and can include an optional title that becomes a caption. ```md ![Doxa screenshot](/screens/screen-1.png "Doxa documentation interface") ``` ![Doxa screenshot](/screens/screen-1.png "Doxa documentation interface") ## Math Inline math works with double-dollar syntax, so you can write search or ranking examples like $$score = relevance / latency$$. Block math uses fenced `math` code blocks: ```math score = \frac{titleMatch + headingMatch + keywordMatch}{\log(contentLength + 1)} ``` ## Authoring Checklist * Use h2 and h3 headings in MDX body content. * Do not repeat the page title as an h1. * Add language identifiers to every code block. * Keep tables narrow enough to scan on smaller screens. * Use MDX components for navigation, warnings, workflows, diagrams, and file structure.

title: MDX Components
description: Use Doxa's built-in MDX components for navigation cards, callouts, steps, tabs, diagrams, and file trees.
content:
Doxa registers a focused component set for documentation pages in `src/lib/components.ts`. These components are available directly in MDX without per-page imports.

## Component Catalog

| Component                        | Use it for                                                       |
| :------------------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `CardGrid` and `Card`            | Navigation, feature groups, related pages, and external links.   |
| `Note`                           | Important callouts, prerequisites, warnings, and success states. |
| `Step` and `StepItem`            | Sequential workflows.                                            |
| `Tabs`                           | Alternative commands, providers, platforms, or languages.        |
| `Mermaid`                        | Architecture diagrams, agent flows, and process charts.          |
| `FileTree`, `Folder`, and `File` | Project structure and content layout.                            |

## Cards

Use large cards for section navigation and compact cards for related links.

```jsx showLineNumbers
<CardGrid>
  <Card
    subtitle="Authoring"
    title="Code Blocks"
    description="Show commands, configuration, and source snippets."
    href="/authoring/code-blocks"
  />
</CardGrid>
```

## Notes

Use notes for information a reader should not miss.

<Note title="Success" type="success">
  Use success notes to confirm expected output or completion states.
</Note>

<Note title="Danger" type="danger">
  Use danger notes for irreversible actions, security risks, or destructive
  commands.
</Note>

## Steps

Use `Step` for workflows that must happen in order.

## Tabs

Tabs are useful when the user chooses one option from a small set.

## File Tree

Use file trees to explain physical structure.

## Mermaid

Use Mermaid when the relationship is easier to understand visually.

## Composition Rules

* Put cards in grids; do not place standalone cards between paragraphs.
* Use steps for sequential work, not feature lists.
* Keep notes short enough to scan.
* Use tabs only when all tab values are mutually exclusive.
* Prefer one Mermaid diagram per page section.

title: Configuration
description: Configure Doxa site metadata, feature toggles, AI providers, analytics, SEO, and content sources.
content:
Doxa configuration is split by concern. Site and SEO defaults live in `src/settings/main.ts`, AI provider settings live in `src/settings/ai.ts`, and content backend selection lives in `src/settings/content.ts`.

## Site Settings

`Settings` controls public metadata, branding, analytics, and feature flags.

```ts {8-12,35-43} showLineNumbers
export const Settings = {
  site: {
    name: "doxa",
    url: "https://docs.example.com",
    description: "Beautiful, fast documentation that stays out of your way.",
    keywords: ["documentation", "mdx", "ai"],
    icon: "/favicon.svg",
  },
  features: {
    branding: true,
    rightSidebar: true,
    feedbackEdit: true,
    tableOfContents: true,
    scrollToTop: true,
    ai: {
      chat: true,
      chatWithPage: true,
      floatingInput: true,
    },
    copyPage: {
      markdown: true,
      rawText: true,
    },
    rawMarkdown: true,
    loadFromGithub: false,
  },
};
```

| Setting group           | Purpose                                        |
| :---------------------- | :--------------------------------------------- |
| `site`                  | Name, URL, description, keywords, and favicon. |
| `company`               | Footer company name and link.                  |
| `seo`                   | Open Graph image, alt text, and social handle. |
| `analytics`             | Google Tag Manager id and enablement flag.     |
| `features`              | Toggles for docs shell behavior.               |
| `openGraph` / `twitter` | Social metadata used by the root document.     |

## AI Provider

The docs chat uses TanStack AI. Configure the provider and model in `src/settings/ai.ts`, then set `AI_API_KEY` in the environment.

<Note title="Secret required" type="warning">
  The chat endpoint returns a configuration error when `AI_API_KEY` is missing.
  Set the key in local `.env` files and in your deployment provider.
</Note>

## Environment Variables

| Variable            |       Required       | Used for                                                       |
| :------------------ | :------------------: | :------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `VITE_SITE_URL`     |      Production      | Canonical URLs, sitemap, Open Graph, and AI-native route URLs. |
| `AI_API_KEY`        | When chat is enabled | Provider authentication for Chat with Docs.                    |
| `CONTENT_SOURCE`    |       Optional       | Selects `vite` or `github`. Defaults to `vite`.                |
| `DOXA_GITHUB_OWNER` |    GitHub source     | Repository owner for runtime docs loading.                     |
| `DOXA_GITHUB_REPO`  |    GitHub source     | Repository name for runtime docs loading.                      |
| `DOXA_GITHUB_TOKEN` | Private GitHub docs  | Read-only token for private docs repositories.                 |

## Sitemap Generation

Production builds generate `/sitemap.xml` through TanStack Start. Doxa seeds
the sitemap from `src/contents/settings/documents.json`; the default Cloudflare
build also prerenders each docs page and keeps link crawling enabled for
internally linked pages.

## Feature Toggle Guidance

* Keep `ai.chat` enabled for demos that showcase the AI assistant.
* Disable `ai.chatWithPage` when readers should use only the main Chat with Docs drawer.
* Disable `ai.floatingInput` when the bottom floating prompt is too prominent for your docs.
* Use `copyPage.markdown` and `copyPage.rawText` to control page-level copy formats.
* Disable `rawMarkdown` if you do not want any docs page exposed as raw `.md` (see [Raw Markdown Routes](/ai/raw-markdown)).
* Disable `feedbackEdit` unless `GitHubLink.href` points at a real repository.
* Keep `tableOfContents` enabled for long reference pages.
* Use `loadFromGithub` as a product-facing toggle only after `CONTENT_SOURCE=github` is configured.

title: Content Sources
description: Choose between bundled Vite documentation and runtime GitHub documentation with Doxa's content adapter layer.
content:
Doxa reads documentation through a single `contentStore` API. The active adapter decides where MDX comes from, while route rendering, AI tools, search-adjacent metadata, and AI-native routes keep the same interface.

## Adapter Options

| Source   | Where content lives                    | Best for                                            |
| :------- | :------------------------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------- |
| `vite`   | `src/contents/docs` in the app repo    | Fast bundled docs shipped with the site.            |
| `github` | A GitHub repository fetched at runtime | Content teams editing docs separately from the app. |

## Vite Source

The Vite adapter is the default. It bundles MDX with the app at build time.

```bash
CONTENT_SOURCE=vite
```

Use this when docs and site code are shipped together.

## GitHub Source

The GitHub adapter fetches MDX through the GitHub REST API and caches results.

```bash showLineNumbers
CONTENT_SOURCE=github
DOXA_GITHUB_OWNER=your-org
DOXA_GITHUB_REPO=your-docs-repo
DOXA_GITHUB_BRANCH=main
DOXA_GITHUB_BASE_PATH=src/contents/docs
DOXA_GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_xxx
DOXA_GITHUB_CACHE_TTL=300
```

## Shared Contract

The adapters implement the same operations:

| Method               | Returns                                     |
| :------------------- | :------------------------------------------ |
| `listFiles()`        | All MDX paths relative to the docs root.    |
| `readFile(filePath)` | Raw MDX for a file, or `null` when missing. |
| `name`               | Adapter name for logs and debugging.        |

## Choosing A Source

## Operational Notes

* Search data is generated from local MDX during build-time workflows.
* Runtime GitHub content is ideal for route rendering and AI-native reads.
* Keep folder structure consistent across sources.
* Document custom adapters if you add R2, KV, Supabase, Notion, or another backend.

title: Customization
description: Customize Doxa branding, theme, colors, icons, MDX components, navigation, and metadata.
content:
Doxa is designed to be edited directly. Most customization happens through settings files, registered components, and static assets rather than a plugin system.

## Customization Map

| Area                                    | File                                                              |
| :-------------------------------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Site name, URL, metadata, feature flags | `src/settings/main.ts`                                            |
| Navigation and GitHub link              | `src/settings/navigation.ts`                                      |
| Theme choice and primary color          | `src/contents/settings/theme.json`                                |
| Logo assets                             | `public/doxa-logo-*.svg`, `public/logo.svg`, `public/favicon.svg` |
| Icon aliases for MDX cards              | `src/settings/icons.ts`                                           |
| MDX component registry                  | `src/lib/components.ts`                                           |
| Component usage guidance                | `agent/component-playbook.md`                                     |

## Branding

Update the site name and metadata in `Settings`.

```ts {3-7} showLineNumbers
export const Settings = {
  site: {
    name: "Acme Docs",
    url: "https://docs.acme.com",
    description: "Technical documentation for Acme developers.",
    keywords: ["acme", "api", "developer docs"],
    icon: "/favicon.svg",
  },
};
```

## Theme And Color

The root route loads `theme.json`, resolves the selected theme, and injects CSS variables for the active font and primary color.

```json showLineNumbers
{
  "activeTheme": "default",
  "primaryColor": "zinc"
}
```

## Icons

Cards accept icon keys from `src/settings/icons.ts`.

```ts showLineNumbers
export const iconMap = {
  alignJustify: LuAlignJustify,
  arrowUpRight: LuArrowUpRight,
};
```

Then use the key in MDX:

```jsx
<Card
  title="Navigation"
  href="/navigation"
  icon="alignJustify"
  variant="small"
/>
```

## Register MDX Components

Add MDX-available components to `src/lib/components.ts`.

```ts {8-9} showLineNumbers
export const components = {
  a: RoutedLink,
  Card,
  CardGrid,
  FileTree,
  Mermaid,
  Note,
  Step,
  StepItem,
};
```

## Customization Workflow

title: Deployment
description: Deploy Doxa Docs to Cloudflare Workers, Vercel, or Docker with the right build target, environment variables, and validation steps.
content:
Doxa includes scripts for Cloudflare Workers, Vercel, and Docker. Cloudflare is the default target, Vercel has dedicated build and deploy commands, and the included Dockerfile makes Doxa Docs deployable to any VPS or server that supports Docker with a single build-and-run flow.

## Deployment Targets

## Required Production Settings

| Setting             | Why it matters                                                                                            |
| :------------------ | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `VITE_SITE_URL`     | Preferred host for canonical tags, sitemap entries, robots, Open Graph URLs, and `llms.txt` source links. |
| `AI_API_KEY`        | Required when Chat with Docs is enabled.                                                                  |
| `CONTENT_SOURCE`    | Selects bundled or runtime docs content.                                                                  |
| GitHub adapter vars | Required only when `CONTENT_SOURCE=github`.                                                               |

## Cloudflare Workers

## Vercel

## Docker

Because the repository ships with a production Dockerfile, Doxa Docs can be
deployed to any VPS or container host that supports Docker without writing a
custom server wrapper. Build the image, run it behind your provider's reverse
proxy, and point your domain at the host.

<Note title="Minimal runtime" type="info">
  Docker uses Nitro's Node server preset and a non-root distroless Node 24
  runtime image. The final image contains only Nitro's generated `.output`
  directory, not source files, package manager state, or development
  dependencies. Seeing `Listening on: http://localhost:3000/` in the container
  logs is expected when the host is mapped with `-p 6000:3000`.
</Note>

## Preflight Checklist

* \[x] `pnpm generate-content-json` has been run after content changes.
* \[x] `src/contents/settings/documents.json` has no stale demo routes.
* \[x] `VITE_SITE_URL` points at the production docs URL.
* \[x] `AI_API_KEY` is set when chat is enabled.
* \[x] `/sitemap.xml` is generated and uses the production docs host.
* \[x] `/llms.txt` and `/llms-full.txt` return the expected content.
* \[x] Search opens with `Command+K` or `Ctrl+K`.
* \[x] Docker deployments map a host port to container port `3000` and pass secrets as runtime environment variables.

title: Architecture
description: A guided tour of the Doxa Docs template — routes, content layer, sidebar, search, AI surfaces, and how MDX components are registered.
content:
The template is a TanStack Start app on Vite, with MDX content compiled at build time and a pluggable content adapter for runtime reads.

## High-level shape

```text
Browser ──► TanStack Router ──► /docs/$.tsx (catch-all)
                                  │
                                  ├─ MDX module from import.meta.glob
                                  ├─ Sidebar    (per-section routes)
                                  ├─ Breadcrumb (per-section trail)
                                  └─ Pagination (prev/next within section)
```

Server-only routes (`/llms.txt`, `/llms-full.txt`, the AI chat agent) read content through the same `contentStore` that the build-time search indexer uses. The renderer never touches `fs` directly.

## Where to find things

## Subsystems

### Sections

Configured in `src/settings/sections.ts`. Exactly one section is `default: true` and lives at `/<page>`; non-default sections own a folder named after their slug and live at `/<slug>/<page>`. The `<SectionTabs />` component reads this list and hides itself entirely when only the default section exists.

### Content store

`src/lib/content/store.ts` exposes an async, edge-safe API (`getAllPaths`, `getEntry`, `readRaw`, `searchContent`). The adapter is selected from `src/settings/content.ts` via `CONTENT_SOURCE` (`vite` or `github`). The agent tools (`grep`, `cat`), `/llms.txt`, `/llms-full.txt`, and the search indexer all consume `contentStore` — they never call `fs` directly. This is what makes the template runnable on Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge, and Docker's Node server target.

### Page routes

`src/lib/pageroutes.ts` flattens each section's nav file (`documents.json`, `documents.<slug>.json`) into a per-section route map. Helpers: `getRoutesForSection(slug)` returns the full nav (headings, spacers, items); `getPageRoutesForSection(slug)` returns just the linkable pages.

### MDX rendering

`src/routes/$.tsx` resolves the splat to an MDX module via `import.meta.glob('/src/contents/docs/**/index.mdx', { eager: true, import: 'default' })`. The available components are registered in `src/lib/components.ts`.

### Sidebar

`src/components/sidebar/index.tsx` derives the current section from the pathname (`getSectionFromPath`) and feeds the section's routes into `<PageMenu />`. `<SubLink />` renders the optional `icon:` from frontmatter via `getIcon()` from `src/settings/icons.ts`.

### Search

Build-time indexer at `src/scripts/content.ts` walks every MDX file, strips custom JSX components, builds a MiniSearch index with per-entry `section` metadata, and writes `public/search-data/index.json`. The client (`src/components/navigation/search.tsx`) hydrates the index on idle and renders section filter chips when more than one section exists.

### AI surfaces

`/llms.txt` and `/llms-full.txt` group entries by section using the same `contentStore`. The chat agent's system prompt (`src/lib/agent/system-prompt.ts`) enumerates configured sections so the agent can scope its answers.

## Adding new MDX components

<Note title="Keep the playbook current" type="warning">
  The playbook is consumed by the Doxa CLI agent for documentation generation.
  Out-of-date docs there produce out-of-date generated content.
</Note>

## Next

title: Coding Standards
description: The conventions every change to the Doxa template is reviewed against — TypeScript, ESLint, Prettier, and import ordering.
content:
These are the rules that pre-commit hooks and CI enforce. When in doubt, run `pnpm clean` and let the tooling decide.

## TypeScript

* **Strict mode is on.** `tsconfig.json` enables `strict`, `noUnusedLocals`, `noUnusedParameters`, and `noFallthroughCasesInSwitch`.
* Prefer `interface` for public component props; use `type` for unions, intersections, and small ad-hoc shapes.
* Avoid `any`. Use `unknown` when you genuinely don't know the shape, then narrow.
* Re-export types from their nearest module — don't deep-import from a sibling's internals.

```ts
// Good
import type { SearchResult } from "@/src/lib/search";

// Avoid
import type { SearchResult } from "@/src/lib/search/types";
```

## Path aliases

| Alias  | Resolves to    | Use for                                            |
| :----- | :------------- | :------------------------------------------------- |
| `@/*`  | `./` (root)    | Anything outside `src/`, e.g. `@/utils/foo.ts`     |
| `~/*`  | `./src/*`      | Optional shorthand for `src/` imports              |

In practice the codebase uses `@/src/...` everywhere — it's the consistent style and works in both client and edge runtimes.

## ESLint

`pnpm lint` runs `eslint . --quiet`. The config uses `@eslint/js`, `typescript-eslint`, and a few project-specific rules. Highlights:

* Unused locals / params are errors. Prefix intentionally unused params with `_` only when an interface forces them.
* React 19 is the target — no need for `import React from "react"` in JSX files.
* No console logs in shipped code paths. Build/script files (`scripts/**`, `src/scripts/**`) are exempt.

Run autofix before staging:

```bash
pnpm lint:fix
```

## Prettier + import order

Import sorting is handled by `@ianvs/prettier-plugin-sort-imports`. The plugin enforces grouped, alphabetised imports — don't fight it. Run:

```bash
pnpm format
```

Tailwind class ordering is also Prettier-managed via `prettier-plugin-tailwindcss`. Keep classes inline; don't sort them by hand.

## Tailwind & UI conventions

* Tailwind 4 is in use — config-less, driven by the `@tailwindcss/postcss` and `@tailwindcss/vite` plugins.
* Reach for **Radix primitives** before building from scratch (`@radix-ui/react-dialog`, `react-tabs`, etc.).
* Icons come from `react-icons/lu`. Reusable icons get registered in `src/settings/icons.ts` so they're available by name in frontmatter and in the `Card` component.
* Classnames merging uses `cn()` from `@/src/lib/utils` — always prefer it over manual template strings when conditional.

```tsx
import { cn } from "@/src/lib/utils";

<button className={cn("rounded px-3 py-1", isActive && "bg-primary text-white")} />
```

## Server-safe code

Some routes run on the edge (Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge). Two rules:

1. **Don't import `fs`, `path`, `child_process`, or other Node-only modules from app code.** Use `contentStore` from `src/lib/content/store.ts` instead.
2. **Build-time scripts are exempt.** Anything under `scripts/**` or `src/scripts/**` runs in Node and can use the standard library freely.

## Comments

Default to writing none. Add a comment only when the **why** is non-obvious — a hidden constraint, a workaround for a known bug, or a decision a future reader would otherwise reverse. Don't narrate **what** the code does.

```tsx
// Good — captures a non-obvious constraint.
// `import: "default"` skips the sibling `__rawSource` export injected by
// mdxSourceCapturePlugin so raw MDX strings stay out of the client bundle.
const mdxModules = import.meta.glob(/* … */);

// Avoid — restates the code.
// Loop through the routes and render each one.
```

## Pre-commit hook

Husky runs `lint-staged` on every commit. It autofixes lint and formatting on the staged files. **Don't bypass it** with `--no-verify` — if the hook fails, fix the underlying issue and commit again.

## Next

title: Contributing
description: How to propose changes to the Doxa Docs template — issues, branches, commits, pull requests, and the review flow.
content:
We welcome bug fixes, small features, and documentation improvements. Larger changes are easier to land if you open an issue first to align on direction.

## Before you open a PR

## Branching

Branch off `main`. Use a focused, descriptive name:

```text
fix/sidebar-active-style
feat/api-reference-section
docs/contributing-guide
chore/bump-tanstack
```

Keep one logical change per branch. If you discover unrelated work along the way, open a follow-up branch instead of bundling it.

## Commits

The repo follows **Conventional Commits**. Recent history:

```text
fix: docs responsiveness
chore: cleanup
feat: improved tightened chat agent...
```

Commit types we use:

| Type       | Use for                                                          |
| :--------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `feat`     | A user-visible feature or capability                             |
| `fix`      | A user-visible bug fix                                           |
| `docs`     | Changes to the documentation content or generator               |
| `chore`    | Tooling, deps, lockfile, config — no behaviour change            |
| `refactor` | Internal restructure with no behaviour change                    |
| `perf`     | A measurable performance improvement                             |
| `style`    | Formatting / whitespace only                                     |

The first line is the headline (under ~70 chars, present tense, no trailing period). Use the body for the **why**, not the **what** — the diff already shows what.

```text
feat: add multi-section docs (Documentation / Development)

Render top-level section tabs above the sidebar so a single site can
host Documentation alongside API Reference and Development guides.
Single-section sites are unaffected — tabs auto-hide.
```

## Opening a pull request

A good PR description is short and answers four questions:

1. **What changed?** One or two bullets.
2. **Why?** The problem or motivation. Link the issue.
3. **How to test?** A concrete checklist someone else can follow.
4. **Anything risky?** Edge cases, follow-ups, intentional out-of-scope items.

Suggested template:

```markdown
## Summary
- Bullet describing the change
- Bullet describing user-visible behaviour

## Motivation
<!-- Why is this change worth making? Link the issue. -->

## Test plan
- [ ] `pnpm quality` passes
- [ ] `pnpm build` succeeds
- [ ] Manually verified <specific path> in the dev server

## Notes / follow-ups
<!-- Optional: known limitations, deferred work -->
```

## Review flow

* A maintainer reviews within a few business days for small changes; larger ones take longer.
* Address feedback by **pushing additional commits** to the branch. Don't force-push during review unless you're rebasing on `main` or asked to squash.
* Once approved, a maintainer merges. Squash-merge is the default — your headline becomes the merge commit.

## Documentation changes

If your change adds or modifies an MDX component, a frontmatter field, or a section, also update **`agent/component-playbook.md`** in the same PR. The CLI agent reads it at runtime — out-of-sync docs there cause bad generated content downstream.

If your change adds or removes a script in `package.json`, update [Local Setup](/development/setup) so the daily-commands table stays accurate.

## Reporting bugs

Bugs are tracked on GitHub. A useful report includes:

* The version / commit hash you reproduced on
* Exact steps to reproduce
* What you expected vs. what happened
* Browser + OS (for UI bugs); deploy target (for build / runtime bugs)

A minimal repro (a fork with the failing case isolated) drastically shortens the fix loop.

## Next

description: How to clone the Doxa Docs template, run it locally, navigate the codebase, and contribute changes back.
content:
This section is for engineers who want to **work on the template itself** — fix bugs, ship features, or extend the platform — rather than authors who only want to write docs. If you're here to write content, head to [Authoring → Markdown](/authoring/markdown).

## What's in this section

## How sections work

The page you're reading lives inside the **Development** section. Sections are configured in `src/settings/sections.ts` and rendered as the tab bar above the sidebar. The current setup:

| Section         | Slug            | Folder                                 | URL prefix                  |
| :-------------- | :-------------- | :------------------------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| Documentation   | `documentation` | `src/contents/docs/` (rootless)        | `/<page>`              |
| Development     | `development`   | `src/contents/docs/development/`       | `/development/<page>`  |

Each non-default section gets its own nav file: `src/contents/settings/documents.development.json`. The default section keeps `documents.json` unchanged.

title: Quality And Testing
description: The verification gates a change must pass before review — typecheck, lint, format, build, and the manual smoke checks the template needs.
content:
There's no unit-test suite for the template itself — verification is structural (typecheck, lint, build) plus manual smoke checks against the running app.

## The pre-PR gate

Run this before opening a pull request. It's the same gate CI runs.

```bash
pnpm quality
```

`pnpm quality` chains:

| Step         | Command             | What it catches                                   |
| :----------- | :------------------ | :------------------------------------------------ |
| Format check | `pnpm format:check` | Prettier drift, unsorted imports                  |
| Lint         | `pnpm lint`         | Unused symbols, banned APIs, accessibility lapses |
| Typecheck    | `pnpm typecheck`    | Cross-file type errors (`tsc --noEmit`)           |

If `format:check` fails, run `pnpm clean` (autofix lint + format) and try again.

## Build verification

Always do at least one production build before merging anything that touches:

* routes / loaders
* the content store or its adapters
* the docs generator
* any `import.meta.glob` site

```bash
pnpm build
```

The build prerenders all static doc pages. Any glob mismatch, SSR-only `fs` usage, or broken adapter shows up here, not in `pnpm dev`.

## Manual smoke checks

For non-trivial UI changes, walk through the golden paths in the running app:

## Search and AI surfaces

After meaningful content edits, regenerate the search index and visit the AI routes:

```bash
pnpm generate-content-json
```

Then check:

* `/search` — visit and run a couple of queries.
* `Cmd+K` palette — open from anywhere; section filter chips should appear when more than one section exists.
* `/llms.txt` — entries grouped under each section's label.
* `/llms-full.txt` — full corpus, sectioned the same way.

## When to skip a check

You don't need a full `pnpm build` for **content-only** changes (edits inside `src/contents/docs/**/*.mdx` with no new components). `pnpm generate-content-json` plus `pnpm typecheck` is enough.

For **code changes**, run the full gate. Skipping the build is the most common reason a PR breaks deployment.

## Performance budgets

There's no automated budget yet, but be deliberate about:

* Bundle size in the client — avoid pulling Node-only utilities into route components. The CI/build will fail fast if you do, but it's cheaper to notice yourself.
* Eager `import.meta.glob` calls — they bundle every match into the entry chunk.

title: Release Process
description: How merged changes become a published version of the Doxa Docs template — versioning, tagging, and deploying.
content:
The template ships as a public repository — there's no npm package to publish. A release is a tagged commit on `main` plus a deploy of the showcase site.

## Versioning

We follow **Semantic Versioning** (`MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`):

| Bump  | When                                                                               |
| :---- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| MAJOR | Breaking changes to file structure, frontmatter shape, or required configuration   |
| MINOR | New capability that's backwards-compatible (a new section, new MDX component, etc.) |
| PATCH | Bug fixes, copy edits, dependency bumps with no behaviour change                   |

The current version is in `package.json#version`. Bump it in the PR that lands the change, not in a separate "version bump" PR.

## Cutting a release

## Deploying the showcase

The showcase site (the public preview of the template) deploys automatically from `main` to Cloudflare. Manual deploys, if you need them:

<Note title="Don't deploy from a feature branch" type="warning">
  Production deploys come from `main`. Use preview environments (Cloudflare
  Pages preview, Vercel preview deployments) for branch-scoped verification.
</Note>

## Changelog conventions

Group entries under the version heading by type:

```markdown
## v1.2.0 — 2026-04-30

### Added
- Multi-section docs with top-level tab bar
- Optional per-page `icon:` frontmatter field

### Changed
- Sidebar now renders only the routes for the active section

### Fixed
- Pagination prev/next now stays within the active section
```

Keep entries user-focused. Internal refactors only get a line if they affect users (build size, deploy targets, etc.).

## Post-release

* Watch the showcase deploy logs for prerender errors.
* Open a fresh "next release" tracking issue if you have a backlog of follow-up work.
* If you broke something, ship a patch release the same way — don't amend the tag.

## Next

title: Local Setup
description: Clone the Doxa Docs template, install dependencies, run the dev server, and learn the scripts you'll use day-to-day.
content:
A clean checkout to a running dev server takes about a minute on a warm pnpm cache.

## Prerequisites

* Node.js `^24.x` (the engines field enforces this)
* pnpm `10.25.0` or newer (declared in `package.json#packageManager`)
* Git

## Clone and install

## Daily commands

| Command                        | What it does                                                        |
| :----------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `pnpm dev`                     | Run the dev server (Cloudflare target by default).                  |
| `pnpm build`                   | Produce a production build for the active deploy target.            |
| `pnpm start`                   | Preview the production build locally.                               |
| `pnpm typecheck`               | Run `tsc --noEmit` across the workspace.                            |
| `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix`  | ESLint (read-only / autofix).                                       |
| `pnpm format` / `format:check` | Prettier (write / verify).                                          |
| `pnpm quality`                 | Format check + lint + typecheck — the gate before opening a PR.     |
| `pnpm clean`                   | Run `lint:fix`, `format`, then `format:check` to tidy your changes. |
| `pnpm generate:docs`           | Append newly added MDX pages to the matching nav file.              |
| `pnpm generate:docs:watch`     | Same, but rerun on file changes.                                    |
| `pnpm generate-content-json`   | Rebuild the search index from current MDX content.                  |

## Adding pages while you work

Drop a new folder under `src/contents/docs/<page>/index.mdx` (or `src/contents/docs/<section-slug>/<page>/index.mdx` for a non-default section), then run:

```bash
pnpm generate:docs
```

The generator is **append-only** — it never reorders or deletes nav entries. It does, however, sync the optional `icon:` frontmatter field on existing entries so sidebar icons stay current.

## Environment variables

Most local development needs none. The two that affect behaviour:

* `DOXA_DEPLOY_TARGET` — `cloudflare` (default), `docker`, or `vercel`. Picked up by `pnpm dev:*` and `pnpm build:*`.
* `CONTENT_SOURCE` — `vite` (default) or `github`. Selects the content adapter in `src/settings/content.ts`.

## Next

title: Navigation
description: Control sidebar order, page headings, spacers, and generated navigation metadata in Doxa.
content:
Doxa separates page discovery from navigation design. MDX files live in folders, while `src/contents/settings/documents.json` controls the sidebar order, section headings, spacers, and page titles shown in the docs shell.

## Navigation Model

Each route entry maps to a folder under `src/contents/docs`. The app prefixes these hrefs with `/docs` at render time.

```json showLineNumbers
[
  { "heading": "Start Here" },
  { "title": "Overview", "href": "/overview" },
  { "title": "Quickstart", "href": "/quickstart" },
  { "spacer": true },
  { "heading": "Authoring" },
  { "title": "Markdown", "href": "/authoring/markdown" }
]
```

| Entry            | Purpose                                                        |
| :--------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `heading`        | Adds a non-clickable section label to the sidebar.             |
| `title` + `href` | Adds a clickable docs page.                                    |
| `spacer`         | Adds visual separation between groups.                         |
| `noLink`         | Keeps a page in the route data without rendering it as a link. |

## Folder Structure

The physical folder names become the URL path. A page at `src/contents/docs/ai/chat-with-docs/index.mdx` resolves to `/ai/chat-with-docs`.

## Adding A Page

## Generator Behavior

`pnpm generate:docs` discovers new MDX pages and appends missing entries to `documents.json`.

## Navigation Checklist

* \[x] Every visible page has a route entry.
* \[x] The first route is the page linked by the home page "Get Started" button.
* \[x] Section headings describe user intent, not file layout.
* \[x] Stale demo pages are removed from `documents.json`.
* \[x] Search data is regenerated after large content changes.

title: Overview
description: See how Doxa Docs handles rich MDX authoring, AI chat, fast search, flexible content sources, and edge-ready deployment.
content:
Doxa is a production-ready documentation template for teams that want the docs site, authoring system, and AI experience to feel like one product. It pairs TanStack Start, React 19, Tailwind CSS v4, TypeScript, MDX, and TanStack AI with a focused set of documentation primitives.

## What Doxa Ships

| Capability            | What it gives you                                                                 |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **MDX documentation** | Markdown plus React components registered in `src/lib/components.ts`.             |
| **AI chat**           | A streaming chat drawer that searches and reads the docs before answering.        |
| **Search**            | Fuzzy search backed by generated page metadata, headings, snippets, and keywords. |
| **AI-native routes**  | `/llms.txt` and `/llms-full.txt` for external AI tools and code assistants.       |
| **Content adapters**  | Bundle docs with Vite or fetch them from GitHub at runtime.                       |
| **Edge deployment**   | Cloudflare Workers by default, with Vercel support included.                      |
| **Customization**     | Theme, color, logo, metadata, icons, navigation, and MDX registry settings.       |

## System Flow

The important detail is that Doxa's rendering, search, AI agent, and AI-native exports all read from the same content access layer. Change the content source once and the rest of the app keeps the same contract.

## Best Fit

Doxa is a good fit for:

* Product documentation with code-heavy guides.
* API or SDK docs that need great code blocks and predictable navigation.
* Internal engineering handbooks that benefit from fast search.
* AI-ready knowledge bases where `/llms.txt` and chat are first-class.
* Teams that want content in GitHub but still need a polished reading experience.

## Where To Go Next

title: Project Structure
description: Understand the Doxa folder layout, content pipeline, MDX registry, and generated assets.
content:
Doxa keeps the authoring surface small: MDX content goes in one tree, reusable components live in predictable folders, and generated data is committed under `public/search-data`.

## Important Paths

## How Content Moves

## Source Of Truth

| Area            | Source file                            | Notes                                              |
| :-------------- | :------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------- |
| Page content    | `src/contents/docs/**/index.mdx`       | Markdown and MDX examples.                         |
| Sidebar order   | `src/contents/settings/documents.json` | Curated manually for order and headings.           |
| MDX components  | `src/lib/components.ts`                | Registers what MDX can render.                     |
| Search metadata | `public/search-data/documents.json`    | Generated from MDX content.                        |
| AI behavior     | `src/lib/agent/system-prompt.ts`       | Constrains the assistant to documentation content. |
| Content backend | `src/settings/content.ts`              | Selects Vite or GitHub content.                    |

## Naming Rules

* Use folder names for stable URL slugs.
* Use frontmatter titles for human-readable page names.
* Keep example pages focused on one job.
* Prefer section names based on user intent: "AI & Search" is clearer than "Internals".

title: Quickstart
description: Install Doxa Docs, run the development server, edit your first MDX page, and verify the generated search data.
content:
Use this guide to get from a fresh clone to a running Doxa site with editable content and working search metadata.

## Install And Run

## Edit Your First Page

Documentation pages live under `src/contents/docs`. Every page is an `index.mdx` file inside a folder that becomes the URL slug.

```markdown showLineNumbers
---
title: First Guide
description: A first guide written in Doxa.
keywords: ["guide", "mdx", "doxa"]
---

This page supports **Markdown**, `inline code`, and MDX components.

<Note title="Success" type="success">
  You are writing Doxa documentation.
</Note>
```

## Refresh Search Data

Search, table of contents, and page metadata are derived from `public/search-data/documents.json`. Regenerate it after meaningful docs changes.

```bash
pnpm generate-content-json
```

## Common Commands

| Command                      | Use                                                          |
| :--------------------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------- |
| `pnpm dev`                   | Start local development using the default Cloudflare target. |
| `pnpm build`                 | Create a production build using the default target.          |
| `pnpm start`                 | Preview a production build locally.                          |
| `pnpm generate-content-json` | Rebuild the search and metadata JSON.                        |
| `pnpm generate:docs`         | Append newly discovered MDX pages to `documents.json`.       |
| `pnpm lint`                  | Run ESLint.                                                  |

## Next Steps

title: Search
description: Understand Doxa's generated search index, fuzzy matching, highlighted snippets, and keyboard search flow.
content:
Doxa includes a search modal for fast page discovery. It reads generated metadata from `public/search-data/documents.json`, ranks results client-side, and highlights matching terms in snippets.

## Search Experience

* Press `Command+K` on macOS or `Ctrl+K` on Windows and Linux.
* Type at least three characters.
* Press `Enter` to open the first result.
* Click any result to navigate to that page.

## Index Pipeline

## What Gets Indexed

| Source                    | Search use                               |
| :------------------------ | :--------------------------------------- |
| Frontmatter `title`       | High-weight result title.                |
| Frontmatter `description` | Result context and metadata.             |
| Frontmatter `keywords`    | High-weight matching terms.              |
| h2 headings               | Table of contents and relevance signals. |
| Body content              | Snippet extraction and general matching. |
| Inline code and bold text | Additional extracted keywords.           |

## Regenerate Search Data

```bash
pnpm generate-content-json
```

The script parses MDX, removes component wrappers from indexed prose, extracts headings and keywords, and writes the combined JSON file.

## Ranking Model

Doxa combines title, heading, keyword, exact content, and proximity signals. A simplified form looks like this:

```math
score = \frac{title + headings + keywords + exactMatches + proximity}{\log(contentLength + 1)}
```

## Content Tips

* Use reader-language titles such as "Chat With Docs" instead of internal names like "Agent UI".
* Put synonyms in `keywords` when a feature has multiple names.
* Keep descriptions specific because they appear in search and metadata.
* Use h2 headings for major topics users may search for.

## Search Troubleshooting

| Problem                    | Fix                                                      |
| :------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------- |
| New page does not appear   | Add it to `documents.json` and regenerate search data.   |
| Result title is wrong      | Fix the page frontmatter title.                          |
| Search finds stale content | Run `pnpm generate-content-json`.                        |
| Relevant page ranks poorly | Improve headings, keywords, and repeated topic language. |
